Syntactic Structure & Grammatical Form: Fixed interrogative tag clause with negative copular element and discourse-final confirmation marker.
Figurative Meaning (Contextualized Defin: Isn’t that true? Used to seek agreement, underline shared knowledge, or gently challenge someone’s claim.
Usage Contexts: daily speech; family talk; storytelling; humor; community discussion
Cultural Origin or Historical Reference: Common in Gullah discourse as a conversational tag, reflecting West African-influenced pragmatic patterns of call-and-response and communal confirmation.
First Example Sentence: De crab done hide een de grass, enty ain’ so?
Second Example Sentence: Dis chile shum mo dan e tell, enty ain’ so?
Third Example Sentence: Unnuh gwine come back befo daak, enty ain’ so?

Idiom (Gullah): Tek time an’ crack ya teet’
Syntactic Structure & Grammatical Form: Imperative verb phrase joined by coordination; idiomatic action command with body-part noun object.
Figurative Meaning (Contextualized Defin: Take your time and laugh; do not rush past joy, humor, or the sweetness of the moment.
Usage Contexts: humor; family gatherings; encouragement; storytelling; social advice
Cultural Origin or Historical Reference: Rooted in porch talk, praise-house fellowship, and family gatherings where laughter, patience, and oral storytelling shape community life.
First Example Sentence: Come ya, tek time an’ crack ya teet’ wid we.
Second Example Sentence: Granny say tek time an’ crack ya teet’, cause trouble ain’ gwine las’ all day.
Third Example Sentence: At de fish fry, e tek time an’ crack ya teet’ till e belly hurt.

Syntactic Structure & Grammatical Form: Simile-based adjective phrase with noun subject, predicate adjective, and comparative prepositional phrase.
Figurative Meaning (Contextualized Defin: Someone speaks charmingly, persuasively, or flatteringly, sometimes to win favor or hide an agenda.
Cultural Origin or Historical Reference: Draws on Sea Island sugarcane, syrup-making, and food metaphors used to evaluate character, persuasion, and social intent.
First Example Sentence: E mout’ sweet like cane syrup, but e han’ tight wid de money.
Second Example Sentence: De man mout’ sweet like cane syrup when e want de boat.
Third Example Sentence: Don’ trust ebry word; she mout’ sweet like cane syrup.

Syntactic Structure & Grammatical Form: Possessive noun phrase plus degree adverb and adjective; elliptical idiom describing habitual action.
Figurative Meaning (Contextualized Defin: The person steals, takes what is not theirs, or meddles beyond proper boundaries.
Non-Idiomatic Equivalent (Plain, Neutral: That person steals or interferes too much.
Usage Contexts: warnings; workplace; household; gossip; moral correction
Cultural Origin or Historical Reference: Uses body-part metaphor common in African Atlantic speech communities, where the hand symbolizes agency, theft, reach, and social boundaries.
First Example Sentence: Watch de basket, cause e han’ too long.
Second Example Sentence: Dey ain’ lef’ e by de cash box; e han’ too long.
Third Example Sentence: Mauma say don’ run wid dem boy; one a dem han’ too long.

Idiom (Gullah): De day clean, but de trouble still dirty
Syntactic Structure & Grammatical Form: Coordinated contrastive clause with metaphorical adjectives; proverb-like balanced construction.
Figurative Meaning (Contextualized Defin: Outward appearances may look calm or respectable while problems remain unresolved beneath the surface.
Non-Idiomatic Equivalent (Plain, Neutral: Things look fine, but the problem is not solved.
Usage Contexts: politics; family conflict; workplace; literature; social commentary
Cultural Origin or Historical Reference: Reflects Gullah proverb style, linking natural imagery of morning light with moral insight about hidden conflict and unresolved community tension.
Phonetic Transcription (IPA, Gullah): /də deː kliːn bət də ˈtrʌbəl stɪl ˈdɜti/
First Example Sentence: De meeting smile plenty, but de day clean, de trouble still dirty.
Second Example Sentence: After de storm, de day clean, but de trouble still dirty een de yard.
Third Example Sentence: Dey shake han’, yet de day clean, but de trouble still dirty.

Idiom (Gullah): Put ya foot een de pot
Syntactic Structure & Grammatical Form: Imperative verb phrase with possessive body-part object and locative prepositional phrase.
Figurative Meaning (Contextualized Defin: Cook extremely well or add special skill, spirit, and flavor to food or a task.
Non-Idiomatic Equivalent (Plain, Neutral: Do it with excellent skill and flavor.
Usage Contexts: food; praise; family events; church dinners; craftwork
Cultural Origin or Historical Reference: Tied to Lowcountry foodways, communal cooking, and praise for skillful preparation of rice, seafood, greens, and one-pot meals.
First Example Sentence: Auntie put ya foot een de pot wid dis okra soup.
Second Example Sentence: When e mek de perloo, e sho put e foot een de pot.
Third Example Sentence: Who cook dem greens? Somebody put ya foot een de pot.

Syntactic Structure & Grammatical Form: Proverbial negative declarative clause with personified noun subject and passive-like result predicate.
Figurative Meaning (Contextualized Defin: People who never speak up, ask, or advocate for themselves may not receive help or opportunity.
Non-Idiomatic Equivalent (Plain, Neutral: You need to ask for what you need.
Cultural Origin or Historical Reference: Echoes Southern and African diasporic proverbial traditions emphasizing self-advocacy, communal negotiation, and practical wisdom.
First Example Sentence: Tell de teacher wha hunnuh need; shut mout’ don’ git fed.
Second Example Sentence: E miss de chance cause shut mout’ don’ git fed.
Third Example Sentence: Mauma say ask fair an’ plain, cause shut mout’ don’ git fed.

Idiom (Gullah): De basket ain’ know how heavy till e full
Syntactic Structure & Grammatical Form: Personified negative clause with temporal subordinate clause; proverb-like metaphor using household object.
Figurative Meaning (Contextualized Defin: People often do not understand the weight of responsibility until they carry the full burden.
Non-Idiomatic Equivalent (Plain, Neutral: You understand responsibility only after experiencing it.
Usage Contexts: parenting; leadership; work; community service; moral teaching
Cultural Origin or Historical Reference: Grounded in basketry, market work, farming, and carrying traditions in Gullah communities, where daily objects encode lessons about labor and responsibility.
Phonetic Transcription (IPA, Gullah): /də ˈbæskət eːn noʊ haʊ ˈhɛvi tɪl i fʊl/
First Example Sentence: Now e run de shop, e know de basket ain’ know how heavy till e full.
Second Example Sentence: De new captain learn de basket ain’ know how heavy till e full.
Third Example Sentence: Don’ laugh at Mauma work; de basket ain’ know how heavy till e full.

Idiom (Gullah): Moon ain’ shame fa shine pon crab hole
Syntactic Structure & Grammatical Form: Negative personified clause with infinitival purpose phrase and locative complement.
Figurative Meaning (Contextualized Defin: Truth, grace, or dignity can appear even in humble, poor, or hidden places.
Usage Contexts: literature; sermons; encouragement; cultural reflection; social justice
Cultural Origin or Historical Reference: Blends coastal ecology, nighttime fishing imagery, and spiritual reflection common in Gullah storytelling, praise-house language, and Sea Island landscape metaphors.
Phonetic Transcription (IPA, Gullah): /muːn eːn ʃeːm fə ʃaːn pɔn kræb hoʊl/
First Example Sentence: Don’ shame ya small house; moon ain’ shame fa shine pon crab hole.
Second Example Sentence: De chile come from little, but moon ain’ shame fa shine pon crab hole.
Third Example Sentence: In de praise house, e say moon ain’ shame fa shine pon crab hole.

Idiom (Gullah): Trouble got root like live oak
Syntactic Structure & Grammatical Form: Declarative metaphor with possessive verb and comparative prepositional phrase.
Figurative Meaning (Contextualized Defin: A problem is deeply established, hard to remove, and connected to older causes.
Usage Contexts: history; politics; family conflict; land issues; community analysis
Cultural Origin or Historical Reference: References live oaks of the South Carolina Lowcountry, trees associated with endurance, land memory, and long histories of settlement, enslavement, resistance, and kinship.
First Example Sentence: Dis land fuss ain’ new; trouble got root like live oak.
Second Example Sentence: Dey talk quick fix, but trouble got root like live oak.
Third Example Sentence: E family pain stay deep, cause trouble got root like live oak.
